next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

Vel woke him before dawn by shoving the heel of her hand into his shoulder and saying, "Don't sit up fast."

Kem sat up fast anyway. Cold air hit the sweat dried into his shirt. For one confused second he thought they were under attack already. Then he followed her gaze to the dead fire.

At the edge of the ash bed, where they'd buried the coals under dirt before sleeping, a boot print pressed clean through the gray crust into damp black underneath. It belonged to neither of them.

Kem crawled closer on stiff elbows. The tread was narrow through the heel and dense at the ball, made for grip on wet ground, not the soft corridor leather he'd worn out of the Vault and not the secondhand road boots rubbing his heels raw now. He knew that pattern anyway. Hoarded enforcement kept their field boots on racks outside the retrieval offices with little waxed tags tied to the laces, each pair catalogued, maintained, and almost never dirtied.

"How long ago?" he asked.

"Night." Vel was already pulling the cook pot into her pack. "No dew settled in it."

"So they stood over us while we slept."

"Or one of them did." She kicked loose dirt over the print, not because hiding it mattered now but because she did not leave useful things lying around. "Move."

Kem got to his feet too quickly. Yesterday's climb had gone into his calves and stayed there overnight. The muscles answered his urgency with a bright, contemptuous cramp. He bent, breathed through it, and reached for the pack.

Vel caught his wrist before his hand closed on the strap. "Check the perimeter first."

"For what...
open_hooks_to_carry: Vel's disgust at Kem's wastefulness — 286 after mere days
The terms: she guides, he follows, she controls information flow
What Vel conceals: the mechanism's specific requirement for a high-level spender
prev_tail_excerpt:
...a second one did. Kem stood. Calves complained. The blister had soaked through his sock, and wet fabric slid against raw skin with each step. He left the banquet to the ants, the birds, and anything else with a better claim on seven levels' worth of lunch. South was the direction the path offered, so south he went.

The pine forest thinned. Trees gave way to scrubby grassland. Shade gave way to full sun. Heat hit him clean and merciless the moment the last pines fell behind. His neck was already pink. His face felt tight. Out here the air tasted drier, full of dust and pollen, nothing like the Vault's managed atmosphere. At the edge of awareness the Ledger kept humming, quieter now by twelve points. The sensation was hard to name, a fullness slightly less full, like a room with a few books pulled from the shelf. Kem barely noticed it.

South stayed south. His body did what it could, which was less than he would have liked and more than he had any right to expect from a body that had spent twenty-six years doing nothing. Feet bled. Shoulders ached from the unfamiliar rhythm of swinging his arms. Sun caught the signet and flashed. Kem turned the crest inward against his palm and kept walking until the metal bit. Every few steps the thought of stopping came up. Each time he saw the Conservatory bench, his father's open eyes, and chose the road instead.

Behind him, from the pines, birds had started screaming at one another over seven levels' worth of lunch. Sugar and roast fat rode the heat after him. Kem did not turn around. He kept walking until the road took the smell away.

setups_to_plant.description: Vel's careful assessment of Kem's remaining levels and her private satisfaction that he solves her problem — reads as pragmatic alliance-building, later revealed as calculated manipulation

state_deltas_required: Mutual need and mutual disgust between two people who represent opposite failures of the Ledger system
Uneasy alliance formed; Vel has knowledge, Kem has levels, neither trusts the other
